content.value: Now let's look at the practical commands you need to execute for each of the storage concepts we discussed.

items: Run a container interactively: docker run -it --name test_container ubuntu bash
Create a file inside the container: touch /sample.txt
Exit the container: exit
Delete the container: docker rm test_container
Run a new container: docker run -it ubuntu bash
Check for the file: ls /sample.txt (It will be missing)
Create a Docker volume: docker volume create myvol
Run container with volume attached: docker run -it -v myvol:/data --name test_vol_container ubuntu bash
Create a file in the volume path: touch /data/test.txt
Exit and delete the container: exit and then docker rm test_vol_container
Run a new container using the same volume: docker run -it -v myvol:/data ubuntu bash
Check for the file: ls /data/test.txt (The file still exists!)

content.value: Conclusion: Docker Volumes provide persistent data storage.

items: Identify a host folder, e.g., /Documents/project
Run container linking the host folder: docker run -it -v /Documents/project:/app ubuntu bash
Create a file in the host folder.
Check inside the container: ls /app (The file appears instantly in the container)

content.value: Conclusion: Bind Mounts create a direct link between host and container directories.

items: Run a container with a tmpfs mount: docker run -it --tmpfs /temp --name test_tmpfs ubuntu bash
Create a file in the temp folder: touch /temp/secret.txt
Stop and start the container: exit then docker start -i test_tmpfs
Check for the file: ls /temp/secret.txt (The file disappears)

content.value: Conclusion: Tempfs provides fast, temporary, memory-only storage.
